speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you want to give? So we're going to start with you retired and you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tikvah, with organiza--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Breder, tell me what year you retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us a little more about Tikvah. What do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well then, you know, it's about the same time, the same year. I, I, you know, we went in Adath Israel to a lecture. From Israel was here, Mr. [? Klaviter ?], who was the founder of a, of a, of a organization which helps survivors, you know, supports survivors, and sees, you know, that they receive the, the, the, the, the treatments and, and the help what they need and what they, what we earned, I mean, since I am one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but he wanted, Mr. [? Klaviter, ?] only to have here a, you know, a part of [NON-ENGLISH], which will help the Israeli organizations. But we there quite a few survivors. Laura [? Shelley ?] was there. And then was there-- I can't recall the names, quite a few-- Odette Meyer and Rita Cohen. And we talked to each other. And we said, listen, I think so, we would have to do something here for the survivors who live here in San Francisco in the Bay area. So we started it. And how is it different than what the Jewish Family Service offers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's entirely different. We spend our energy and our, our, our-- I mean-- I-- I don't know the term for it-- to help of the survivors. Organization which we founded wants, and wanted always, to be the speaker, and helper, and see that we are not shortcut, that we get the help what we need.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of special needs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Psychological-- we have social workers who go and visit them. We have psychologists and psychiatrists, you know, of our list. And those are volunteers, because we are very well-- organization which we need-- you know, a nonprofit-- which we need money to do more. And it's very hard to get that. So we, ourselves, support that mostly this organization. We meet-- before, we met more often. But now, you know, we are not getting younger. It's harder for us to meet. But at least, once a month, twice a month, once in two months, we meet. But we have now much help from the Jewish Family Service, since we got a grant from the Claim Conference. And the grant says only for survivors. So they have-- now, we work closely with Jewish Family Service to help remaining survivors in, in, in anything what we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever gone into schools to speak, Mrs. Breder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1972, I visit schools and talk about my experience. I have hundreds and hundreds of letters, and only high schools or universities, private schools. And it has been, for me, really something which I wanted to do, to spread the word what happened to us. And for the students, to-- what I get, you know, when I talk to them, when they hear a real survivor speaking, they say how many films I saw. I read books about it and documentation, no comparison. Because they have it first hand. And such a good respond from students, which gives me some, some satisfaction that, somehow, I can do something that never, ever again happened to human beings what did to us, regardless to race or religion. And I hope so it never will. My message to everyone who will see that tape, would like to say, never give up. If you are in some trouble, persecuted with someone unfairly, just keep fighting it, and never give up your faith. And I hope so never, ever again nobody will experience what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I say? Be alive and kick. Keep kicking. I want to thank you very much, Mrs. Breder, for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure. Even, you know, I very often cry from deep scars in my heart. It never will go away. I breathe Auschwitz. I live with Auschwitz bitterness for the rest of my life. Even I know I'm fortunate to be here and to be in a free country. But you know, the 20 years in communism, I-- I-- I told was [? such ?] yuck, capitalism. I enjoy capitalism.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much, Mrs. Breder.

speaker: interviewee
text: Welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandmother, paternal side, the mother of my father. And the picture was taken in 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our hometown, Stropkov. This is Usher Reich, my father's grand-- father. And it was taken in Stropkov, in the town where he was born, in 1914. This is my father, Heinrich Chaim Reich, born in Stropkov, in the town where we lived. And the picture was taken approximately in the '30s. I don't know exact time. This is the wedding picture of Fred Breder and Libusha Reich, taken in August, 1946, in Bratislava. This is a picture taken in Auschwitz in 1944, where I was assigned to work in a working unit, which was called Canada, sorting belongings from people who were killed in the gas chambers of Auschwitz-Birkenau. This is a picture taken in 1956 in Auschwitz between the barrack 10, 11, where the Black Wall was. My husband, Fred Breder, in the front, taking a wreath to the wall in memory of the slaughtered people. This is the [INAUDIBLE], our marriage license from 194-- August, 1946, made in Kosice. The photograph is taken by the Magazine. And it was in the German magazine when I was at the trial in Wuppertal. In the front, you can see me. And in the back, the man is the accused war criminal, SS guard, [? Gottfried Wiser. ?] In 1987 was the trial. And ended, he was sentenced to life in prison. Linda Breder enters the court in Wuppertal in 1987, where the accused SS guard, [? Gottfried Wiser, ?] was sentenced to life in prison. In the forefront, you see me, Linda Breder, in 1987, in-- in Dussledorf, at the airport. And beside me is the welcome committee from the city of Wuppertal, senators and these both. I don't know their names anymore. This is the marriage, marriage license from my parents, which married in 1914 in my home town, in Stropkov. But it was signed some other city very close to. There are the names of my grandparents and my parents and the year when it was when they married. This is a picture made in Los Angeles at the Simon Wiesenthal Center, where I and my sister, who is next to me, Edith Velisch, and then, Mrs. Zaplethal, three-year Auschwitz survivor, and Martha Weil, three years, a three years Auschwitz survivor, presented to the museum a book which called, The man standing there receiving the picture book is a representative of the Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles. I forgot the name. This is our-- those are our children and grandchildren. First is Linda, my granddaughter, who is now a student in Arizona at the University of Tempe. Then is our grandson, Elliot, who attends the Hillel Brandais School in San Francisco, our daughter, my daughter, Dasha, and David, our son-in-law and her husband together. It was made in 1996. This is our daughter-in-law, Carina. They have a little son, Ethan, also a student at the Hillel Brandais in San Francisco. And our son, Paul, Pavel, or Chaim in Hebrew, made in 1996. Those three are our grandchildren. Caroline-- that's our son's daughter-- Steven, also our grandson from my son, and Linda, my daughter's daughter. So altogether, we have five grandchildren and two great grandchildren, which we show in the next picture. Continue. This is a family picture of the Breders. The first one is our grandson, Steven, which is holding our great grandson, Richard. The next is Carina, our daughter-in-law, with her little boy Ethan, Pavel, Paul, Chaim, our so, our granddaughter, Caroline, with her daughter, Christina. my beloved husband, Fred, Fridrich. And next to him, my beautiful face, Libusha, Linda, American, Linda, my granddaughter, Elliot, my grandson-- they're brother and sister-- then my daughter, Dasha, and our son-in-law, her husband, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was made quite a while ago, maybe eight years ago. And now you see the whole [? my ?] family. And I think so, I don't know, maybe more pictures continue. Who knows?
